title: "Very fast and chaotic but people are valued."
review:
On the forefront; transparency from Dario and Daniela.  Culture and values are so focused on that we generally hire people who want to help each other (low ego and high humility).  People are there for the mission and not themselves which is very rare in tech. Chaotic - trying to do everything all at once so many conflicting priorities.
Some leaders need to get a backbone to say no and manage out under performing people.

title: "Problems as we scale, but 3 years in and still the best place I’ve worked"
review:
- Incredible autonomy to do the work you feel is most important
- mission alignment is a real thing. If you’ve never worked at a company driving toward an outcome bigger than “making money,” then I don’t think you’d really understand what this means or feels like, but it’s probably why retention is so high. 
- high trust in colleagues and founders. The founders make principled decisions. I trust them more than any other company leadership I’ve had in the past.  I think DDJ do a good job stress testing and pushing back on each other to come to a decision - Some teams have scope creep, some teams don’t have clear deliverables, some teams don’t really make sense at all 
- has become more political overtime, but I don’t know if that’s avoidable 
-some of the executives seem unqualified for their roles mostly because they’ve never done this before and/or don’t seem to know what they’re doing

title: "Things are changing"
review:
The company provides office meals for breakfast lunch and dinner as well as massages Teams are growing and scaling but we’ve seem to lost company culture and good talent. Lots of work politics with strong opinionated members that claim credibility due to their prior work roles and titles. No clear guidance from senior management and we’re constantly rushing projects to scrap.
Comp sounded great but no one gets rewarded past their initial package with no transparency on leveling and adjustments.

title: "Plan to be here for a while"
review:
Brilliant, collaborative colleagues with low egos. Incredible amount of transparency on day 1. Truly innovative work.

A few additional thoughts/anecdotes:
- This is the first workplace I've been at where our culture does not feel like a facade. Actions from leadership are aligned with their words. The "culture" session during onboarding was great (my favorite is "Hold light and shade") and I believe in the culture more every day.
- On work-life balance, I work on the evenings/weekends because I feel motivated to, not because my boss wants me to, so it feels more like play than work. I am also encouraged to take time off.
- You do not have to be AGI-pilled (I'm not), but you do have to be open minded and willing to have debates / conversations. My interview experience was poor. It was slow, and my recruiter was not responsive. In fact, it was so bad that I initially turn down the offer for a competing lab. Now that I'm on the other side, I can see the *volume* of candidates we receive, and have a bit more understanding of the pressure recruiting is under. That said, I still think we need to dramatically up our recruiting game to attract the best talent in the industry.

title: "This is the place to be"
review:
There are people here that are so so talented and energetic. Overall everyone is just super excited to be here and very humble and kind at the same time. Some of the best culture of any company I’ve been at.

Leadership is extremely thoughtful & transparent about every decision they’re making and clearly is not overly driven by profits. So far it’s been very fun to work here and I’ve been given latitude to pick projects that are interesting to me. Being able to use cutting-edge AI tools on the job also makes it easier to spend more time on the fun stuff. Expectations are quite high. I would not work here if you want great WLB but FWIW I would def take 60hrs/wk of exciting and impactful work over 30hrs/wk of sleepy boring work.

title: "Obviously the place to be"
review:
- Incredible people, talent density is off the charts and continues to grow.
- Extremely close knit, collaborative, not political at all, strong "we're in this together" culture
- Management is reasonable, listens to folks, very strong company culture of speaking up, questioning norms, getting and giving feedback
- Tons to do - low hanging fruit everywhere. More problems than we have people. It's very easy to work on extremely high impact stuff.
- More remote friendly than other AI labs, offices in SF, NY, London, Seattle, etc
- Cushy job - still tons of benefits and nice office perks - reminds me of Google/FB before the money ran out
- We have the best models and none of the drama :) we've had way more people leave from That Place to This Place than the reverse direction - Growing quickly: some of the things that made Anthropic so special may not be true in the future, though so far we've dealt with the growth reasonably well
- Less clear career progression: company is new and the "we're in this together" culture means that people are mostly focused on making sure AI and Anthropic goes well, rather than focusing a lot on individual career growth and progression. People who are used to level-chasing at Big Tech won't find much of that here, for better or for worse. We are growing quickly and therefore have a plurality of newer/somewhat less experienced managers who are managers simply because they were early employees, and thus may not be calibrated on broader industry norms
- Sometimes chaotic - obviously AI is a very fast-paced space, this means we often pivot, thrash, which segues into
- WLB: We obviously work very hard here. Plenty of individual freedom to work and set your own schedule, but be prepared to sprint when necessary.
- If you work at Anthropic you may become convinced that you are working at the most important thing, at the most important time, at the most important company, perhaps in all of history. This may or may not be true—at the very least, take it with a grain of salt.
